title: Resources
text:
Searching for statistical data privacy resources? You’re in the right place. Here we provide a range of resources, from CRAN packages to influential articles.
We are actively looking to update these resources. Please contact us if you have suggestions, or submit a pull request.

title: Resources
text:
Articles

A Statistical Framework for Differential Privacy (2012) by Larry Wasserman and Shuheng Zhou
Advances and Open Problems in Federated Learning (2021) by Peter Kairouz et al.
Balancing Data Privacy and Usability in the Federal Statistical System (2022) by V. Joseph Hotz et al.
Disclosure Control and Random Tabular Adjustment (2018) by Mark Stinner
Federated Learning: Challenges, Methods, and Future Directions (2020) by Tian Li et al.
How Statisticians Should Grapple with Privacy in a Changing Data Landscape (2020) by Joshua Snoke and Claire McKay Bowen
Policy Impacts of Statistical Uncertainty and Privacy (2022) by Ryan Steed et al.
Privacy-Preserving Data Analysis for the Federal Statistical Agencies (2017) by John Abowd et al.
Statistical Data Privacy: A Song of Privacy and Utility (2023) by Aleksandra Slavković and Jeremy Seeman

title: Resources
text:
Books and Book Chapters

Statistical Disclosure Control for Survey Data (2009) by Chris Skinner
Protecting Your Privacy in a Data-Driven World (2021) by Claire McKay Bowen

title: Resources
text:
CRAN (R) Packages

acro: A Tool for Automating the Statistical Disclosure Control of Research Outputs
AQuadtree: Confidentiality of Spatial Point Data
cellKey: Consistent Perturbation of Statistical Frequency and Magnitude Tables
diffpriv: Easy Differential Privacy
duawranglr: Securely Wrangle Dataset According to Data Usage Agreement
easySdcTable: Easy Interface to the Statistical Disclosure Control Package ‘sdcTable’ Extended with Own Implementation of ‘GaussSuppression’
pda: Privacy-Preserving Distributed Algorithms
ppmf: Read Census Privacy Protected Microdata Files
ppmHR: Privacy-Protecting Hazard Ratio Estimation in Distributed Data Networks
PPRL: Privacy Preserving Record Linkage
RegSDC: Information Preserving Regression-Based Tools for Statistical Disclosure Control
sdcHierarchies: Create and (Interactively) Modify Nested Hierarchies
sdcLog: Tools for Statistical Disclosure Control in Research Data Centers
SDCNWay: Tools to Evaluate Disclosure Risk
sdcSpatial: Statistical Disclosure Control for Spatial Data
sdcTable: Methods for Statistical Disclosure Control in Tabular Data
synthpop: Generating Synthetic Versions of Sensitive Microdata for Statistical Disclosure Control
uwedragon: Data Research, Access, Governance Network : Statistical Disclosure Control

title: Resources
text:
Python Packages

Google’s Differential Privacy Libraries
Diffprivlib: General-Purpose Library for Experimenting with, Investigating, and Developing Applications in Differential Privacy
OpenDP: The Core Library of Differential Privacy Algorithms Powering the OpenDP Project
PipelineDP: Python Framework for Applying Differentially Private Aggregations to Large Datasets Using Batch Processing Systems such as Apache Spark, Apache Beam, and More
PySyft: Perform Data Science on Data that Remains in Someone Else’s Server
PyDP: OpenMined’s Python Differential Privacy Library
Tensorflow Privacy: Library for Training Machine Learning Models with Privacy for Training Data
Tumult Analytics: Tumult Labs’ Differential Privacy Library, running on PySpark

title: Resources
text:
Additional Resources
If you have additional resources that you’d like to share on this page, join our group and let us know on our discussion forum.

title: About
text:
We are the Privacy & Confidentiality Interest Group (PCIG) of the American Statistical Association (ASA), a community of both members and non-members of the ASA who are united by our interests in statistical theory, methodology, and applications related to privacy and confidentiality. Our group embodies a wide range of active professional interests within the ASA’s purview and fosters an international community of statisticians. More information about our group’s objectives and structure can be found in our Charter.

Membership Information
We welcome anyone interested in the field of statistical data privacy and confidentiality. Our members are a diverse group of individuals from various sectors, including industry, government, and academia. To join us, please visit our Join page.


Elected Officers (2026)

Chair: Carlos Soto
Chair-Elect: Joshua Snoke
Program Chair: Gary Howarth
Program Chair-Elect: Matthew Williams
Communications Chair: James Bailie
Secretary: Minsun Riddles

Stay tuned for a call for nominations to the 2026 leadership committees!


Leadership Committees (2025)







Committee
Members

Nominations/Elections
Aref Dajani (Chair), Matt Williams, Monika Hu


Student Outreach/Young Professionals
Zeki Kazan (Chair), Alyssa Columbus, Joshua Snoke





About the ASA
The ASA, established in 1839 in Boston, is the second oldest professional association in the United States that is still in operation. It is recognized as the largest global community of statisticians, often referred to as the “Big Tent for Statistics.” The ASA is dedicated to promoting excellence in the creation, application, and distribution of statistical science. This is achieved through various means such as organizing meetings, publishing research, providing services to members, offering education, granting accreditation, and advocating for the field.
In addition to these, the ASA also provides professional development programs for early-career statisticians and data scientists. It offers a platform for its members to connect with a global community of statisticians working in various sectors including industry, government, and academia. These members contribute to advancing research, promoting sound statistical practice, informing public policy, and improving human welfare.

title: News
text:
Stay updated with the latest developments in our interest group and the contributions of our members to the statistical data privacy landscape. From new papers to insightful talks, find out what’s new in our community.
Our Google Group (accessible by members only) has additional news, updates and discussions from PCIG.

Feed



Date/Event
News Item




Elections 2025 – Results
We are happy to announce the following election results and congrats to elected officers: Joshua Snoke (Chair-Elect); Matthew Williams (Program Chair-Elect); and James Bailie (Communications Chair). Thank you for casting your votes during the PCIG 2025 election. Thanks also to our Elections Committee Member, Monika Hu, for organizing the vote.


Elections 2025 – Now Open
Voting for PCIG officer positions is currently open. PCIG members should have received a link to vote via email. (Please contact us if you haven’t.) Voting closes November 21. We will be calling for nominations to our various committees soon.


Elections 2024 – Results
Congratulations to our new officers: Saki Kinney (Chair); Carlos Soto (Chair-Elect); Michael Hawes (Program Chair); Gary Howarth (Program Chair-Elect); and Minsun Riddles (Secretary). Thanks to our Elections Chair, Aref Dajani, for organizing the vote.


JSM 2023
The ASA Privacy & Confidentiality Interest Group, initiated by Saki Kinney and Claire McKay Bowen, was approved by the ASA Council of Sections Governing Board.

title: Events
text:
Don’t miss out on our upcoming events! Whether we’re hosting, sponsoring, or attending, we’d love for you to join us. Check out the table below for more details.
If you know an event which is of interest to the PCIG community, please get in touch with us so we can add it to this page.

Upcoming Events



Date
Event




March 11-12, 2026
The 2026 Federal Committee on Statistical Methodology (FCSM) Conference at the Suitland Federal Center in Suitland, MD. As part of this conference, the PCIG is organising a mixer at Kirwan’s on the Wharf for March 12 starting at 5:30 – see here for more details.


May 1, 2026
NYC Privacy Day 2026, an in-person event organized and hosted this Spring by the NYU Tandon Center for Responsible AI (NYU R/AI).


June 1-2, 2026
TPDP 2026 (Theory and Practice of Differential Privacy), Boston. This year there will be a special session on Differential Privacy for Health and submissions aligned with this theme are especially encouraged. The deadline to submit a 4 page abstract is February 18, 2026 AoE.


June 24-25, 2026
The 8th Annual PrivaCI Symposium, University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign. The aim of this symposium is to foster interaction among diverse communities of research and practice using Contextual Integrity to reason about privacy, and to design and evaluate, craft regulation, and generate formal logics for privacy. The deadline for submissions is February 13, 2026 AoE.


August 1-6, 2026
JSM 2026: Details on privacy and confidentiality related sessions to come.





Past Events



Date
Event




February 9-10, 2026
The 2026 Privacy and Public Policy Conference (PPPC) program committee is now accepting submissions. Following the success of the inaugural PPPC in 2024 this event will bring together privacy experts, researchers, data stewards, data practitioners, and public policymakers. See here for further information. The deadline for submissions is December 15, 11:59 PM EST, 2025.


January 28, 2026
SoDa Symposium In Celebration of Privacy Week: “Balancing Statistical and Non-Statistical Uses of Federal Data: Privacy, Governance, and Public Trust,” with speakers Dr. Sallie Ann Keller, Michael B. Hawes, and Alexandra Wood (online). See here for more details and to register.


August 2-7, 2025
JSM 2025: There will be 22 sessions related to privacy and confidentiality, including an introductory overview lecture on data privacy and statistical science to be presented by Jerry Reiter and Claire Bowen. The PCIG JSM mixer will be held Tuesday, August 5 at 5:30PM in the Omni Hotel Room H-Cumberland 3. Come learn about PCIG and mingle with colleagues! Also stop by the PCIG community table #8 in the Expo. We look forward to seeing you there!


September 13-14, 2024
Join us for the inaugural Privacy and Public Policy Conference to be held September 13 and 14, 2024 at the Georgetown Massive Data Institute in Washington, DC. The program will include a range of speakers, presentations, and networking opportunities all focused on the intersection of privacy law, privacy technology, and public policy challenges. For more details, visit https://privacypublicpolicy-conference.github.io/website/.


August 6, 2024
JSM 2024: PCIG Program Chair Michael Hawes has compiled a list of 24 sessions related to privacy and confidentiality. Our JSM Mixer will be held Tuesday August 6th, 4pm at the Steeplejack Brewery, located at 2400 NE Broadway, Portland, OR 97232. Come learn about the new ASA Privacy and Confidentiality Interest Group, mingle with colleagues, and enjoy some food and drink in a cool local brewery! More details in the flyer.

title: Welcome!
text:
Welcome to the official website of the Privacy & Confidentiality Interest Group of the American Statistical Association (ASA PCIG). Our community is comprised of committed researchers and practitioners with a shared interest in the field of statistical data privacy and confidentiality. We aim to introduce statisticians and others to this rewarding field and foster collaborative methodological development.

We Look Forward to Collaborating With You!

To learn more about our initiatives and how you can contribute, please visit our About page.
Membership to the PCIG is free and open to anyone. (You do not need to be a member of the ASA.) Join here!
Members can stay up to date with the PCIG community in our Google Group. We encourage all members to post discussions, news and opportunities there.



If you have any questions or suggestions for our group, please reach out to us via our Contact page.

title: PCIG Mixer at the 2026 FCSM Conference
text:
As part of the 2026 Federal Committee on Statistical Methodology (FCSM) Conference, the PCIG is hosting a mixer at Kirwan’s on the Wharf for March 12 starting at 5:30. This will be a great opportunity to learn about the PCIG, meet other members of the community, and enjoy some food and drink in a fun local venue. We hope to see you there!

Details:
We have booked a room located upstairs and will provide some appetizers but people will need to buy their own drinks. The room can hold about 25 people, and any overflow can mingle at the upstairs bar.
Kirwan’s on the Wharf
749 Wharf St SW, Washington, DC 20024
People can take the Metro from Suitland to very close to Kirwan’s. There is also parking at the Wharf or on any of the nearby streets.
Directions from Waterfront Metro Station (Green Line):

Leave from 4th St SW - about 0.1 mi, less than 1 min
Turn right onto M St SW - 0.1 mi, about 2 min
Bear right at Maine Ave SW - 0.2 mi, about 4 min
Turn left onto 7th St SW - about 0.1 mi, less than 1 min
Turn right onto 7th St SW - about 0.1 mi, less than 1 min
Turn right onto Water St SW - about 0.1 mi, less than 1 min
You have arrived at Water St SW. Your destination is on the left
Proceed upstairs to the reserved room
